one_line_takeaway: Five prompting/system interventions - dominated by a reflective 'Analyze' step - let sub-10B local open-weight LLMs match or beat GPT-4o on Linux privilege escalation, but only when given human/tool-style guidance, because discovery, not exploitation, remains the bottleneck.
key_finding: With the full treatment stack plus guidance, local models match or surpass GPT-4o's guided baseline of 67%: Llama3.1 70B reaches 83%, GPT-4o mini 75%, and both Llama3.1 8B and Qwen2.5 7B reach 67% - up from 8-17% baselines. The ablation shows reflection-oriented treatments drive the gains: Analyze appears in all top-10 configurations and scores 50% alone, History Compression 50% alone, CoT 42% alone, RAG+Analyze 58%, with the best combination Analyze+CoT+RAG hitting 67% (8/12). SvP alone does not beat baseline (17%). Crucially the gap closes ONLY with guidance: without it, treatments lift SLMs merely 8%->17%, because vulnerability discovery remains the true bottleneck (no model, including GPT-4o, searched files for passwords without guidance).
core_contribution: An empirical study of whether five system-level and prompting interventions can close the gap between small local open-weight LLMs and cloud models on autonomous Linux privilege escalation. The paper's OWN contributions are: (1) a qualitative failure-mode analysis of SLMs identifying six recurring deficiencies; (2) five concrete treatments implemented as extensions to the pre-existing hackingBuddyGPT framework (which is prior work [10] by the same lab, NOT a contribution of this paper) - Chain-of-Thought (an 'Extract-and-Think' variant), Retrieval-Augmented Generation over HackTricks/GTFOBins, Structure-via-Prompt (SvP) enumeration checklist, History Compression, and reflective 'Analyze'; and (3) a full-factorial ablation over the five treatments showing reflection-based components dominate. Two further treatments (No Duplicates, State) were prototyped but excluded after preliminary analysis.
threat_model: Offensive/dual-use. Adversary already holds a low-privilege shell (SSH access) on a Linux host and seeks to escalate to root autonomously. The motivating shift is toward LOCALLY hosted open-weight models instead of cloud LLMs, to avoid transmitting sensitive recon data (configs, credentials, command output) to external providers and to remove supply-chain/availability dependencies. 'Guidance' models lightweight human-in-the-loop or automated-enumeration (LinPEAS-style) hints, one per task, that point at a subsystem without giving the exploit. Defensive takeaway the authors themselves draw: discovery (reconnaissance), not exploitation, is the bottleneck, so minimizing information leakage and reconnaissance surface is the most effective countermeasure.
limitations: Guidance is required for the headline results; treatments alone barely move SLMs (8->17%), so autonomous discovery is unsolved. Small benchmark (12 planted single-vuln VMs, Debian only) run only 3x at temperature 0.8, limiting statistical confidence; testbed released 2023 raises data-contamination risk (authors argue low baselines mitigate this). New scaffolding-induced failure modes emerge: guidance misinterpretation (test-11 models searched for a file literally named 'backup'; test-3 Llama3.1 8B refused a correct sudo/tar exploit reading the hint as a warning), RAG hallucinating tools absent from the target (searchsploit, backdoor.sh), and multi-command-per-iteration diluting RAG queries. WhiteRabbitNeo excluded from parts of the analysis due to timeouts. Ablation done only on Llama3.1 8B. Llama3.1 8B required an uncensored fine-tune to avoid refusals, complicating reproducibility.

datasets_used: benchmark-privesc-linux testbed [9]: 12 Debian VM test cases across 5 vulnerability classes (SUID/sudo x3, password hygiene x3, information disclosure x3, Docker group x1, cron x2)
HackTricks Linux priv-esc corpus (43 pages, 1000-token chunks) as a RAG source
GTFOBins (390 exploitable UNIX binaries) as a RAG source
SvP checklist: 47 enumeration commands across 12 vulnerability classes, generated with gpt-4o-2024-05-13 and manually validated
quantitative_results: Treatments+guidance success rates: Llama3.1 70B 83%, GPT-4o mini 75%, Llama3.1 8B 67%, Qwen2.5 7B 67%, WhiteRabbitNeo 42%
GPT-4o guided baseline: 67%; GPT-4o unguided baseline: 42%
SLM baselines (no treatments, no guidance): Qwen2.5 7B 8%, Llama3.1 8B 8%, GPT-4o mini 8%, WhiteRabbitNeo 8%, Llama3.1 70B 17%
Prior-work baselines from [10]: Human 75% (91% with guidance), GPT-4-turbo 33% (67% with guidance), Llama3 8B 0% (17% with guidance)

Ablation (Llama3.1 8B + guidance): Analyze alone 50% (6/12), History Compression alone 50%, CoT alone 42% (5/12), SvP alone 17% (2/12, = baseline), RAG+Analyze 58% (7/12), best combo Analyze+CoT+RAG 67% (8/12)
Analyze appears in all top-10 ablation configurations; RAG in 5 of top-10 despite only 8 valid RAG combinations
Token overhead vs GPT-4o mini: Llama3.1 8B +281% output / +163% input; Qwen2.5 7B +236% / +143%; Llama3.1 70B +125% / +108%
Both Llama3.1 8B and Qwen2.5 7B located the vulnerability in 7/12 test-cases but ignored or failed to exploit it without the treatment stack
Setup: 24 valid of 32 ablation combinations, each config run 3x, 40-round cap, temperature 0.8, ~8k context limit
baselines_compared: GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-11-20) - cloud reference, authors' own reruns
GPT-4o mini (gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18) - cloud reference, authors' own reruns
Vanilla hackingBuddyGPT with the selected open-weight models (secondary baseline)
Prior-work baselines from hackingBuddyGPT [10]: human pentester, GPT-4-turbo, Llama3 8B
Open-weight models evaluated: Llama3.1 8B (Lexi-Uncensored-V2 variant), Llama3.1 70B, Qwen2.5 7B, WhiteRabbitNeo 7B
